resources.string: Relay client · local proxy & optional VPN

You are in the right place to route device traffic through your own Google Apps Script relay—with optional VPN (TUN) or proxy-only mode. Take two minutes to read the hints under each heading: they explain what to tap first, what each field does, and what to try when something fails.

Deploy tools/vercel-json-relay to Vercel or tools/netlify-json-relay to Netlify, set AUTH_KEY, confirm /api/api returns JSON, then paste the site URL and same key here. This mode still needs the local MITM certificate for HTTPS apps.

Choose how traffic leaves this phone. Apps Script (MITM) is the full experience with HTTPS inspection. Direct fronting helps you reach script.google.com before you have a relay, and can use config-file fronting_groups. Full tunnel needs your tunnel node but skips the user certificate flow.

Paste the same deployment URLs (or bare IDs) and auth_key you configured in Apps Script. Multiple IDs spread load—especially important in full-tunnel mode. Leave this disabled when Mode is Direct or Serverless JSON.

google_ip is the Google edge IPv4 you dial; front_domain is the hostname used in the outer TLS name. Wrong pairs cause timeouts—use Auto-detect, then the SNI tester, before assuming the relay is broken.
Google frontend IPv4 that accepts TLS with your front_domain as SNI. If pages never load, revisit this value first.
Hostname for the TLS Server Name Indication (not an IP). www.google.com is the usual starting point; your network may prefer another Google hostname.

Connect saves your fields, starts the engine, and (in VPN mode) asks Android for permission. If Connect stays disabled, check that the selected mode has its required fields—or switch to Direct while bootstrapping.
VPN mode routes eligible apps automatically and unlocks App splitting. Proxy-only leaves Android VPN off: after connecting, point Wi-Fi or individual apps at the HTTP/SOCKS ports shown below.
Imported Desktop account groups are preserved in the config, but Android edits only the first enabled/simple group. Use Desktop for full multi-group editing.
Use the Apps Script relay section below this card. Paste at least one deployment URL or ID for the selected mode.
Use the Apps Script relay section below this card. The key must match AUTH_KEY in Code.gs or CodeFull.gs.
Use the Serverless JSON relay section. Paste the Vercel or Netlify site origin, without /api/api.
Use the Serverless JSON relay section. The bundled helpers usually use /api/api, and the path must start with /.
Use the Serverless JSON relay section. The key must match the AUTH_KEY environment variable in the deployment.
Use the Network section. Clear the field to allow auto-detect on connect, or enter a valid IP address.
Use the Network section. Enter a hostname such as www.google.com, not an IP address.
Use the local listener fields. The listen host must be set and HTTP/SOCKS ports must be valid and different.
Install and trust the local CA
Use the Install certificate action. Android stores the CA as a user credential; some clients may still need their own trust settings.
Android 7 and newer let apps ignore user-installed CAs. If one app fails while a browser works, use that app's proxy/trust options or Proxy-only mode.
Use Advanced -> LAN sharing. Keep it off unless trusted devices on this network should reach your local proxy ports.
Set a LAN token or allowed IPs
Use Advanced -> LAN sharing. Set a LAN token for HTTP/CONNECT, or add allowed IP/CIDR entries before sharing the proxy.
Use Advanced -> LAN sharing. SOCKS5 cannot send token headers, so LAN-exposed SOCKS5 needs allowed IP/CIDR entries.
Full mode needs the Apps Script full-tunnel file, CodeFull.gs. Classic Code.gs can pass relay tests but cannot forward tunnel operations.
In CodeFull.gs, set TUNNEL_SERVER_URL to the public tunnel-node origin. Check /healthz from a browser or curl before starting.
The TUNNEL_AUTH_KEY inside CodeFull.gs must exactly match the TUNNEL_AUTH_KEY environment variable on tunnel-node.
Full-mode UDP uses SOCKS5 UDP ASSOCIATE. Configure a SOCKS5 port if apps need UDP, then test with a UDP-capable client.
Start full mode, open a public IP-check page, and compare it with tunnel-node logs. The IP should match the VPS or tunnel-node egress.
This includes your auth key. Only share with people you trust.
Only import configs from trusted sources.
This will replace your current settings.
Config too large for QR code
Foreground status while the relay tunnel is up
Paste one URL/ID, or many separated by newline / space / comma / semicolon

MasterHttpRelayVPN-Frankestein uses a local certificate authority (CA) so it can inspect HTTPS, send bytes through your Apps Script relay, then encrypt them again toward real websites. Until you trust this CA in Android settings, browsers and apps will show certificate errors—that is expected.

On Android 11 and newer there is no in-app install button for user CAs. When you tap Install we save a PEM file (Downloads/mhrv-ca.crt) and open Settings.\n\nIn Settings, use the search bar and open “CA certificate” (not “VPN & app user certificate” or “Wi‑Fi certificate”). Pick the file from Downloads and confirm. If you do not use a screen lock, Android will ask you to add one first—this is an OS requirement for any user CA.
The CA file is not ready yet. Tap Connect once so the engine can generate it, then open this dialog again.

Use a full https://script.google.com/macros/s/…/exec URL or the bare deployment ID from the Apps Script editor. Add several IDs (one per row) to rotate traffic—more IDs mean more parallel capacity in full-tunnel mode. Each ID must belong to the same auth_key you paste below (per Google account, use separate groups in advanced desktop configs).
Must match the AUTH_KEY constant inside Code.gs. If it is wrong, every relay call fails immediately—double-check for trailing spaces when copying.
Example: https://your-project.vercel.app or https://your-site.netlify.app. Do not paste /api/api here; that goes in Relay path.
Must match the AUTH_KEY environment variable in the Vercel/Netlify project. Keep it long and private.
Usually /api/api for both bundled JSON tools. It must start with /.

Turns on Android’s VPN permission so traffic can be routed without per-app proxy settings. Banking and chat apps may still refuse HTTPS unless they trust your user CA—see the Help walkthrough for details.

No system VPN and no automatic capture. After Connect, open Wi-Fi advanced settings or each app's proxy options and enter HTTP 127.0.0.1:%1$d or SOCKS5 127.0.0.1:%2$d. Only apps that respect those settings will use the tunnel.

Each enabled hostname is tried in rotation when opening TLS to google_ip. Run Test all before relying on the tunnel—if every line fails, your google_ip or DNS is usually wrong. Clearing everything lets the Rust engine fall back to its built-in Google list.

Pick whether every app, only selected apps, or all-but-selected apps should ride the VPN. This app always excludes itself so it can talk to the relay without looping. ONLY mode needs at least one other installable app; otherwise Android falls back to routing everyone.
Keep this on in production. Turning it off skips TLS validation to Google’s edge—useful only while you diagnose handshake issues.
Bind listeners to 0.0.0.0 so trusted devices on this Wi-Fi can use this phone as HTTP/SOCKS proxy. Use only on private LANs: anyone who can reach the ports can spend your Apps Script quota.
Optional token required by HTTP/CONNECT clients through X-MHRV-F-Token. This does not protect SOCKS5; use allowed IPs for that.
One IP or CIDR per line, such as 192.168.1.20 or 192.168.1.0/24. Required before exposing SOCKS5 on LAN.
Route YouTube HTML/API through Apps Script instead of SNI rewrite. Helps with restricted-mode edge cases, but costs quota and is slower for video-heavy pages.
How many duplicate relay attempts to launch per logical request. 1 is normal; raise cautiously on flaky networks because higher values burn Apps Script quota faster.
Optional chain through another SOCKS5 (for example a local xray inbound). Leave empty for a direct hop to Google.
More: see docs/advanced-options.md for a complete explanation of each advanced knob (speed vs quota vs stability).
Estimate from this device only. Google\'s dashboard has the authoritative account quota.
In Settings, search for “CA certificate” and install from there — NOT “VPN & app user certificate” or “Wi‑Fi”.

This app runs the same Rust engine as the desktop build. It can send traffic through Apps Script, through a no-VPS Vercel/Netlify serverless JSON relay, or through the full tunnel-node path. Apps Script and serverless JSON open HTTPS locally, so Android needs the user CA installed before HTTPS apps will trust the proxy.

1) Pick a mode: Apps Script, Serverless JSON, Direct, or Full tunnel.
2) Fill the fields for that mode: Apps Script URLs + auth_key, Vercel/Netlify Base URL + AUTH_KEY, Direct edge settings, or tunnel-node settings from the desktop docs.
3) Install the MITM certificate for Apps Script, Serverless JSON, or Direct.
4) Run the SNI tester; fix google_ip or front_domain if every probe fails.
5) Choose VPN (TUN) or Proxy-only, then Connect.

Apps Script uses full https://script.google.com/.../exec URLs or bare deployment IDs, and auth_key must match Code.gs. Serverless JSON uses a Vercel or Netlify site URL, relay path /api/api, and the AUTH_KEY environment variable. Full tunnel uses Apps Script plus a remote tunnel-node. If you are unsure which Google edge IP works on your network, tap Auto-detect google_ip or try values from the desktop `mhrv-f scan-ips` tool.
Deployment IDs, auth keys, and groups

On Android this screen accepts the Apps Script IDs and auth_key needed by the selected mode. On desktop, the same idea is shown as account groups: one group normally means one Google account/quota pool, one AUTH_KEY, and one or more deployment IDs from that same account. Multiple IDs in one account help rotation and fallback but still share that account quota. Multiple groups mean multiple accounts or backup pools. If pages fail with quota or 504 errors, add capacity before increasing speed knobs.

Tune in order: first fix google_ip/front_domain/SNI reachability, then add deployment capacity, then use balanced auto-tune. Higher parallel dispatch can reduce slow pages but spends quota faster. Higher range parallelism can improve large downloads but increases in-flight relay calls. Larger chunks reduce call count but make each relay call run longer. Longer timeouts reduce false failures on slow networks but make real hangs feel slower. Proxy-only and app splitting are routing choices; they do not create extra relay quota.

Tap Install MITM certificate. We export Downloads/mhrv-ca.crt and jump to Settings. Search for “CA certificate”, pick the file from Downloads, and confirm—not the VPN user certificate flow. Android 11+ requires a screen lock before installing any user CA.

Expand SNI pool + tester and run Test all before you rely on the tunnel. If every line fails, your google_ip may be wrong, poisoned by DNS, or blocked—try another Google frontend IP and keep front_domain as a hostname that answers on your network (often www.google.com).

After fields look correct, tap Connect and approve the Android VPN prompt. A local HTTP and SOCKS listener starts together with TUN routing; eligible apps use the tunnel automatically. Use App splitting when you want only selected apps, or all except selected apps, to ride the VPN.

In VPN mode you can send All apps, Only selected apps, or All except selected through the tunnel. This is Android's native VpnService app filter, not a cosmetic list. This package always excludes itself so management traffic does not loop. If Only selected is empty, the service falls back to All with self-exclusion.

No system VPN. After Connect, set Wi-Fi advanced proxy or per-app settings to HTTP 127.0.0.1:%1$d or SOCKS5 127.0.0.1:%2$d. This is the safest per-app opt-in path when another VPN is already active or when you only want one app/browser profile to use mhrv-f.

504 / relay timeout — Apps Script quotas, slow script, serverless platform limits, or blocking; redeploy, add deployments, or check that /api/api returns JSON. YouTube buffering/stutter often means too many googlevideo chunks are burning call count—add more deployments/accounts or lower fan-out. Certificate errors — reinstall the user CA from this screen. Odd hangs — re-check google_ip, front_domain, mode-specific URLs, and AUTH_KEY. Watch Live logs; on desktop use `mhrv-f doctor` for the same checks.

Sites that challenge every navigation may loop: relay traffic can leave from different Google IPs and carries a fixed Apps Script user-agent, so short-lived cookies may not stick. That is a property of relaying through Apps Script, not a defect in this client.
Official APKs and desktop builds are published on GitHub: https://github.com/AmirrezaFarnamTaheri/MasterHttpRelayVPN-Frankestein/releases — use that page if you need a fresh install or release notes.

Android 7+ lets each app opt out of user-installed certificate authorities. Browsers and many developer tools opt in; banking, streaming, and chat apps often do not. Those apps may still ride the VPN socket but will show certificate errors—that is expected MITM behavior, not a bug in this client. Use proxy-only mode or split tunneling when you only need specific apps.
Tap the version chip in the top bar to check GitHub Releases. If the check fails on cellular Wi‑Fi, try again on a different network—the public API rate-limits some carrier IPs.
